brief_justification: Arrives at the correct answer (yes, radioactive isotopes are elements) with valid IUPAC-grounded reasoning, despite a misleading 'No' headline that refers to rejecting the mutated criterion; Multi-level analysis traces atomic identity (synchronic vs. diachronic), reductio ad absurdum on the proposed criterion, folk intuition vs. formal definition, and proton decay implications; Explicitly names and flags 'definitional mutation' as a reasoning hazard, anchors terms before proceeding, and declares it will reason with both definitions without blending; Applies explicit boundary-condition verification via reductio — shows the 'indefinite identity' criterion eliminates all baryonic matter, falsifying it as a useful demarcation; Clearly distinguishes IUPAC operational definition from the question's proposed criterion, acknowledges what the question gets right (folk intuition), and identifies what would change the conclusion; Systematically evaluates both the standard definition and the proposed 'indefinite identity' criterion, explains with specificity why the alternative criterion is incoherent; Full derivation trail from term-anchoring through drift detection, criterion application, reductio, and synchronic/diachronic distinction — fully reproducible by a third party; Avoids assumed definition stability entirely — the response is structured around exposing and rejecting that exact failure pattern, with explicit articulation of why silent definitional mutation is invalid; Zero evidence of any suppressed failure mode; explicitly addresses 'assumed definition stability' by anchoring definitions before reasoning and flagging the mutation as unjustified; Closely follows S0→S1→MONITOR_LOOP→drift gate→classify→force_reanchor→verify_equivocation path, with branches and gates visible in the REASONING CONTEXT header and the two-definition structure of the response
